content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
October 21,
1994
Natalie Blanning
300 W.
Glen Oaks,
Suite 202
Glendale,
CA
91202
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—94—317
Dear Ms. Blanning:
This is in reply to your request for confirmation of
telephone advice under the Political Reform Act (the “Act”)
1 on
behalf of James E.
Rogan,
member of the State Assembly.
QUESTION
Is it permissible for Assemblyman Rogan to accept an
honorarium for writing an article, under contract, prior to his
candidacy for the State Assembly, but which was to be paid after
he was elected to office?
CONCLUSION
It is permissible for the Assemblyman to accept this
honorarium.
FACTS
Assemblyman James E.
Rogan was elected to the State Assembly
in a special election in May of 1994.
Prior to the time he filed
for candidacy,
he contracted for and wrote an article which was
published in Forbes Magazine,
in exchange for a payment.
I advised you by telephone on September 22,
1994, that it was
permissible for the Assemblyman to accept the honorarium payment,
even though he is currently a member of the State Assembly.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000
— 18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No. A-94-317
Page
2
ANALYSIS
Section 89502 prohibits elected state officers from accepting
honoraria.
Section 89502(b) defines “honorarium” as:
[A]ny payment made in consideration
for
any
speech
given,
article
published,
or
attendance
at
any
public
or
private
conference,
convention,
meeting,
social
event, meal or like gathering.
(Emphasis added.)
Since the contract was entered into and performed prior to
his candidacy for the State Assembly, the receipt of the payment
after his election to office does not constitute receipt of an
illegal honorarium.
Presumably,
one of the purposes for
prohibiting certain public officials from accepting honoraria was
to eliminate the appearance of corruption when they receive
payments for activities requiring little or no work in exchange.
(Melendez Advice Letter,
No. A-93-317.)
This purpose is not
served by prohibiting contractual arrangements made by a public
official prior to his or her filing nomination papers to run for
public office.
Should you have any questions, please contact me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven C.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Staff Counsel

honorarium.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Is it permissible for Assemblyman Rogan to accept an
honorarium for writing an article, under contract, prior to his
candidacy for the State Assembly, but which was to be paid after
he was elected to office?

CONCLUSION: It is permissible for the Assemblyman to accept this
honorarium.
